title: Material Attributes, Environmental Factors, and Direct vs. Indirect Methods
narrationScript: Even a perfect eye in perfect light can be defeated by the part itself, by the conditions around it, or by trying to look the wrong way. This lesson covers three things, what the material gives you to work with, what the environment does to the examination, and the two basic ways we get our eyes onto the surface, direct and indirect. Start with the material. The surface condition is the single biggest factor in what you can see. A smooth, clean, low gloss surface shows fine detail well. A rough as cast or as forged texture can hide small indications in its own pattern, and it can also create features that look like flaws but are not. Reflectivity matters too. A dull surface scatters light and reveals relief, while a bright, polished, or wet surface throws glare that can blind you to whatever is under it. Geometry and access decide whether you can even reach the area and get the eye close enough at a useful angle. And the natural color and condition of the material set the contrast you have to work with. Color deserves its own attention. Many of the things an inspector looks for show up as color or a change in shade. Heat tint, the rainbow of temper colors near a weld, tells you about heat and can flag a problem. Corrosion announces itself in reds, browns, greens, and whites. A dye penetrant indication is read by the color of the dye standing out against the developer. Because so much hinges on color, normal color perception is screened when an inspector is qualified, and color critical work needs light that renders colors faithfully. If you cannot trust the color, you cannot trust the call. Now the environment. Cleanliness comes first, because you cannot inspect what you cannot see. Dirt, grease, scale, paint, and loose corrosion all hide the surface, so the right cleaning has to come before the examination, not after. Lighting we have already covered, and it is an environmental factor as much as a tool. Temperature affects both the inspector and the equipment, and extreme heat or cold limits how long and how well you can work. Access and the surroundings shape everything, a confined space, work at height, a cramped corner, or a hot and noisy plant all degrade an examination and raise safety concerns that come before any inspection. Part of being a professional is recognizing when the conditions are not good enough and fixing them before you certify a result. That brings us to the two ways of looking. Direct visual testing means an uninterrupted optical path from the surface to your eye. The widely used geometry is straightforward, get the eye within about twenty four inches, or six hundred millimeters, of the surface, and keep the line of sight at an angle of not less than thirty degrees to that surface. Direct viewing can be helped by simple aids that still keep the light path unbroken, a mirror to see around a corner, a magnifier to enlarge fine detail. Codes commonly treat those mirrors and magnifiers as aids to direct examination. A telescope is treated differently, because it stands in for an eye that cannot get close enough, codes such as ASME list it with the remote viewing aids. Indirect, or remote, visual testing is used when you cannot get your eye to the surface. Here the image is relayed to you by a device, a rigid borescope, a flexible fiberscope, a video borescope with an electronic image sensor in its tip, or a camera carried on a robotic crawler or a drone. The key idea is that the optical path is broken and an instrument carries the view to you. When a remote method stands in for a direct one, the procedure requires it to deliver resolution at least equal to what direct viewing would have given, and that capability is demonstrated, not assumed. Match the method to the access, prepare the surface, control the conditions, and then trust what you see.

sections.heading: Material Attributes: What the Surface Gives You
sections.body:
The condition of the surface is the single biggest control on what an inspector can detect, and it is set by the material and its processing.

- **Texture and finish** - a smooth, clean surface reveals fine detail, while a rough **as-cast** or **as-forged** texture can hide small indications inside its own pattern and even mimic flaws. Machining marks, grinding lines, and weld ripple all add background that the real indication must stand out against.
- **Reflectivity** - a dull, matte surface scatters light and shows relief well; a bright, polished, or wet surface throws **glare** that can blind you to what lies beneath it.
- **Geometry and access** - the shape of the part decides whether you can reach the area and place the eye close enough at a useful angle; tight corners, bores, and overhead positions limit both.
- **Base color and condition** - the natural color and state of the material set the **contrast** available before you ever add light.

Read the surface before you read the indications: a difficult texture or a glossy finish does not excuse a missed flaw, it tells you to change your light, your angle, or your surface preparation.

sections.heading: Color and Contrast
sections.body:
A great deal of what visual testing finds is communicated by **color** or a change in shade, so color is a working tool, not a decoration.

Several conditions are read primarily by color. **Heat tint** - the band of temper colors that forms beside a weld - reports how much heat the metal saw and can flag overheating or sensitization on certain alloys. **Corrosion** identifies itself through reds and browns, greens, and whitish products, and the shade hints at the type. A **liquid-penetrant indication** is read by the contrast of the colored dye, or a fluorescent glow, against the developer background. Even surface films and coatings are judged partly by color.

Because so much depends on it, **color perception is screened** when an inspector is qualified, and it is re-checked on the schedule the written practice sets. Color-critical work also needs lighting that renders color faithfully, since poor color rendering can wash out heat tint or disguise a corrosion product. The practical rule: build **contrast** wherever you can - clean the surface, choose light direction and color quality deliberately - and never make a color-dependent call under light or eyesight you do not trust.

sections.caption: How surface condition changes detectability: the same crack is clear on a clean matte surface but masked by glare and contamination on a glossy, dirty one.

sections.title: Access and safety come before the examination
sections.body: Much VT happens in **confined spaces**, at **height**, near **hot or energized** equipment, or in awkward positions that strain the inspector. These conditions degrade an examination and create hazards that must be controlled - permits, ventilation, fall protection, lockout - **before** any inspection begins. Recognizing when conditions are unsafe or inadequate, and stopping until they are fixed, is part of doing the job correctly.

sections.heading: Environmental Factors at the Surface
sections.body:
Beyond the part itself, the surrounding conditions make or break an examination.

- **Cleanliness** comes first - you cannot inspect what you cannot see. Dirt, grease, scale, paint, and loose corrosion hide the surface, so appropriate **cleaning precedes the examination**, never follows it. Over-cleaning carries its own risk, since aggressive blasting or grinding can smear metal over a crack, so the cleaning method must suit the job.
- **Lighting** is an environmental factor as much as a tool: the available and supplemental light, glare sources, and color quality all act here.
- **Temperature** affects both inspector and equipment; heat, cold, and humidity limit how long and how well anyone can work, and can fog optics.
- **Access and surroundings** - cramped quarters, height, vibration, noise, and a busy operating plant all reduce the quality and reliability of an examination.

The professional habit is to **assess and control the environment first**: clean and prepare the surface, set and verify the lighting, manage temperature and access, and only then begin. Certifying a result obtained under inadequate conditions is how missed indications happen.

sections.heading: Direct Visual Testing
sections.body:
**Direct visual testing** means there is an **uninterrupted optical path** from the examination surface to the inspector's eye. It is the default whenever access allows, because nothing relays or degrades the image.

The widely used geometry is specific and worth memorizing: place the **eye within about 24 in. (600 mm)** of the surface, and keep the line of sight at an angle of **not less than 30°** to that surface. Closer is generally better, and the angle requirement exists because a grazing, near-parallel view loses the detail a steeper look would catch.

Direct examination may be **aided** by simple tools that keep the light path unbroken. A **mirror** lets you see around a corner or into a hidden root; a **magnifier** enlarges fine detail (a 10x hand magnifier is common). Codes such as ASME Section V, Article 9 treat mirrors and magnifiers as aids to **direct** examination, because the inspector is still looking along a continuous optical path - the device only bends or enlarges it, it does not convert the image into a relayed signal. A **telescope** is classed differently: because it stands in for an eye that cannot reach the 24 in. viewing position, ASME lists telescopes with the **remote**-examination aids, alongside borescopes and cameras.

sections.caption: Direct VT geometry (eye within 24 in. and at least 30° to the surface) compared with an indirect, remote setup relaying the image to a monitor.

sections.heading: Indirect (Remote) Visual Testing
sections.body:
**Indirect**, or **remote**, visual testing is used when the eye cannot be placed at the surface at all - inside a bore, deep in a vessel, down a pipe, or anywhere too small, too hot, or too dangerous to enter. Its defining feature is that the **optical path is interrupted** and an instrument carries the image to the inspector.

The common remote tools are the **rigid borescope** (lenses and prisms in a straight tube, for bores and cylinders), the **flexible fiberscope** (an image carried along coherent optical fibers around bends), the **video borescope** (an electronic **CCD or CMOS** image sensor in the tip sending the picture to a screen), and **cameras on robotic crawlers or drones** for tanks, pipelines, and elevated structures. These extend the inspector's reach enormously.

The critical control: when a remote method **substitutes** for direct examination, the procedure requires it to demonstrate **resolution at least equal** to what direct viewing would have provided. That capability is verified - for example against a resolution target at the working distance - not assumed. A blurry screen image is not an acceptable stand-in for an eye on the surface.

sections.caption: Direct versus indirect (remote) visual testing at a glance.
rows: Interrupted; image relayed by a device
Access allows eye within ~24 in. at >= 30 deg
Telescope, borescope, fiberscope, video probe, crawler/drone camera
Meet distance and angle, adequate light
Demonstrate resolution at least equal to direct

interactive.title: Direct or remote? Sort the setups
interactive.intro: Classify each examination setup as direct VT (an uninterrupted optical path to the eye, including mirror and magnifier aids) or indirect/remote VT (a device stands in for an eye that cannot reach the surface). Codes such as ASME Section V, Article 9 treat mirrors and magnifiers as aids to direct examination, and list telescopes among the remote-examination aids.

items.label: Unaided eye held about 18 in. from a weld toe
items.why: An uninterrupted path from surface to eye, within the standard distance, is direct VT.

items.label: Inspection mirror angled to view a hidden weld root
items.why: A mirror keeps the light path to the eye unbroken; it is treated as an aid to direct examination.

items.label: Handheld 10x magnifier held at the surface
items.why: A magnifier enlarges the view without breaking the optical path, so it is a direct-VT aid.

items.label: Small telescope used to read a vessel nameplate high overhead
items.why: The eye cannot reach the 24 in. direct-viewing position; ASME lists telescopes among the remote-examination aids.

items.label: Rigid borescope inserted into a machined bore
items.why: A borescope relays the image through the instrument, so the optical path is interrupted - remote VT.

items.label: Flexible fiberscope passed through an access port
items.why: A fiberscope carries the image along fibers from a place the eye cannot reach - remote VT.

items.label: Video borescope with a CCD sensor in the tip
items.why: An electronic sensor captures the image and sends it to a screen; the path to the eye is broken - remote VT.

items.label: Camera on a robotic crawler driven inside a pipe
items.why: A crawler-mounted camera relays the view from inside the part - remote VT.

checkQuestions.question: Which surface condition generally makes shallow surface relief EASIEST to detect?
options: A bright, polished, or wet surface
A dull, clean, matte surface
A surface coated with oil
A surface left under heavy scale
checkQuestions.explanation: A dull, matte surface scatters light and reveals relief, while polished, wet, oily, or scaled surfaces throw glare or hide the surface, masking shallow features.

checkQuestions.question: Distinguishing the heat-tint (temper) colors beside a weld depends most on which inspector capability?
options: Peripheral (rod) vision
Color perception under color-faithful lighting
Dark adaptation
Maximum magnification
checkQuestions.explanation: Heat tint is read by color, so it requires adequate color perception and lighting that renders color faithfully; this is why color vision is screened during qualification.

checkQuestions.question: A part arrives for VT covered in grease and loose scale. The correct action is to:
options: Inspect through the contamination to save time
Clean and prepare the surface appropriately before examining it
Examine it now and clean it afterward for the photos
Reject the part as un-inspectable
checkQuestions.explanation: You cannot inspect what you cannot see; appropriate cleaning precedes the examination, using a method that does not smear metal over potential cracks.

checkQuestions.question: Widely used direct-VT geometry places the eye and line of sight at approximately:
options: Within 24 in. (600 mm) of the surface and at least 30 degrees to it
Within 6 ft of the surface at any angle
Within 24 in. but only exactly perpendicular (90 degrees)
Any distance as long as the angle is below 10 degrees
checkQuestions.explanation: Common direct-VT practice places the eye within about 24 in. (600 mm) and keeps the line of sight at not less than 30 degrees to the surface; a grazing view loses detail.

checkQuestions.question: Which of these is INDIRECT (remote) visual testing?
options: Using an inspection mirror to see around a corner
Holding a 10x magnifier at the weld
Viewing the inside of a tube on a screen via a video borescope
Examining a weld bead with the unaided eye from about 12 in.
checkQuestions.explanation: A video borescope relays the image electronically, so the optical path is interrupted - remote VT; unaided viewing, mirrors, and magnifiers keep an unbroken optical path and are direct VT.

references: CP-105-2024 VT Topical Outline 3.6 (direct and indirect methods)
CP-105-2024 VT Topical Outline 1.3 / 1.3.2 (test-object characteristics and color)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 9 (direct and remote visual examination, general practice)
